chunk_summary: This section outlines screening triggers for metabolic risk in Indian children using BMI, waist circumference, and clinical risk factors, emphasizing India-specific guidelines and practical triage markers.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of BMI, waist circumference, and clinical risk factors in metabolic risk screening for children.
Learn India-specific BMI cutoffs and waist-to-height ratio thresholds for risk stratification.
Identify high-yield clinical risk factors that warrant metabolic screening in children.
Recognize the benefits of bundled screening approaches to improve efficiency and reduce costs.
key_facts: India-specific BMI cutoffs: ≥23 for overweight and ≥27 for obesity (ages 5–18 years).
For children under 5 years, use WHO weight-for-length/height standards.
Waist-to-height ratio >0.5 is a practical marker for central adiposity risk.
High-yield clinical triggers include family history of ASCVD, type 2 diabetes, maternal gestational diabetes, and signs of insulin resistance (e.g., acanthosis, elevated BP).
Medications like systemic glucocorticoids and atypical antipsychotics can increase metabolic risk.
Bundled screening reduces missed comorbidities, lowers costs, and standardizes follow-up intervals.
reference_quote: "For ages 5–18 years, the IAP approach uses 2015 BMI charts with adult-equivalent cutoffs (about 23 for overweight and 27 for obesity)." (IAP guidelines on childhood obesity, 2023)
document_key_topics: India-specific BMI and waist circumference guidelines
Clinical risk factors for metabolic screening
Bundled screening for metabolic risk in children
